results.content:
# AI Deep Research Is Broken — This Tool Checks the Facts
## Brainqub3
17300 subscribers
12 likes

### Description
225 views
Posted: 23 Oct 2025
Check AI-generated content for hallucinations: [...] if any of it is actually true. And now we're seeing real consequences. High-profile professionals and businesses are beginning to get burned by this blind trust in AI. Let me walk you through three serious cases where AI hallucinations caused reputational and financial damage. First, Deote Australia. They were commissioned to produce a consulting report for the Australian government's Department of Employment and Workplace Relations. But here's what happened next. The report contained multiple errors including a fabricated quotes and references to non-existing academic papers. Deote later admitted that generative AI had been used during the drafting process. The government had to publish a corrected version of the report and Deote agreed to repay a portion of the fee. The whole episode [...] Should we ever trust AI generated research? Now, for some of you, the answer might be an immediate no, but the truth is this question is becoming harder to ignore because AI generated research is everywhere. We're living through a moment where knowledge work has never been cheaper. Generative AI tools like chat GBT, Claude, and Gemini are freely available and widely popular. And with that comes a tidal wave of what I can only describe as research slop. Some of it looks brilliant on the surface. Well written, polished, articulate. So polished in fact that it inspires a sort of faith-like confidence. We read it and just believe it. Worse still, many of us are starting to pass this slop on to customers and clients, often without verifying if any of it is actually true. And now we're seeing

results.title: Brilliant, Broken, and Frustrating: My Deep Dive into Amazon’s Kiro AI IDE, the Flawed Junior Developer - DEV Community
results.content:
Broken:  
 However, I completely resonate with your frustrations regarding the inconsistencies in its performance. As you pointed out, there are moments when the tool appears to misunderstand context or provide subpar suggestions. This can be quite demotivating, especially when time is of the essence.

Frustration:  
 Your commentary on the learning curve is particularly relevant. While the tool has the potential to democratize software development for beginners, the current implementation can feel overwhelming. Junior developers may find it challenging to navigate when Kiro offers multiple suggestions or when it fails to explain its reasoning. [...] The user-reported issues have been widespread and consistent: [...] Stuck Tasks: Tasks frequently get stuck, fail, and require numerous manual retries. I ran into a lot of problems with my terminal that was configured with many plugins; the issue and solution are on GitHub:  The frustration is compounded by the fact that a failed task often loses all context, forcing developers to restart the entire process from scratch and burning through their daily usage limits on unsuccessful attempts. This gap between the product’s promise and its current usability has been a major source of frustration, perfectly captured by my own user’s experience:
